meta.title: STESS — Status Epilepticus Severity Score
meta.authors: Rossetti AO, Logroscino G, Milligan TA, Michaelides C, Ruffieux C, Bromfield EB
meta.outcome: In-hospital survival vs death after an episode of status epilepticus, from four pre-treatment predictors (age, history of seizures, worst seizure type, level of consciousness).
meta.source_note: STESS: Rossetti et al., J Neurol 2008 (doi 10.1007/s00415-008-0989-1, PMID 18769858); score first proposed in Rossetti et al., Neurology 2006 (doi 10.1212/01.wnl.0000223352.71621.97). The most widely externally validated status epilepticus prognostic score (21 validation cohorts pooled in Yuan et al., Epilepsia 2022, doi 10.1111/epi.17442).
meta.cff_info:
What this is. The Status Epilepticus Severity Score (STESS) is a simple bedside score, calculated before treatment, that predicts the chance of surviving an episode of status epilepticus (SE). It is the most widely used and externally validated SE prognostic score.

How it is scored. Four pre-treatment predictors are added — level of consciousness (0–1), worst seizure type (0–2), age (0 or 2) and history of previous seizures (0–1) — for a total of 0–6. A score of 0–2 is favorable; 3–6 is unfavorable.

What the bands mean (Rossetti 2008, 154 patients). A favorable score (0–2) was associated with ~3% in-hospital mortality and an unfavorable score (3–6) with ~39%. Its strength is a very high negative predictive value (0.97): a favorable STESS reliably identifies patients who will survive, and early aggressive coma induction may not be routinely warranted in them.

Important caveat. STESS has a low positive predictive value (0.39) — an unfavorable score does not reliably predict death and, as the authors state, must not be used to justify withdrawal of medical support. Use it to reassure, not to limit care. Population-level estimate; always apply clinical judgement.

source_link.label: PubMed
source_link.title: Rossetti et al., J Neurol 2008 — STESS

predictors.name: Level of consciousness
predictors.assess: Level of consciousness before treatment. Alert, or somnolent/confused (arousable and responsive) scores 0; stuporous (arousable but non-responsive) or comatose scores 1.

predictors.name: Worst seizure type
predictors.assess: The most severe seizure semiology before treatment (worst manifestation). Simple-partial, complex-partial, absence or myoclonic = 0; generalized-convulsive = 1; nonconvulsive status epilepticus in a comatose patient = 2.

predictors.name: Age
predictors.assess: Patient age. 65 years or older adds 2 points.

predictors.name: History of previous seizures
predictors.assess: Any known history of seizures before this episode (a surrogate for acute vs remote aetiology). Yes scores 0; no or unknown scores 1.

model.panel_title: STESS — predicted outcome after status epilepticus

rows.label: STESS 0 — FAVORABLE: ~3% in-hospital mortality; survival highly likely (NPV 0.97)
STESS 1 — FAVORABLE: ~3% in-hospital mortality; survival highly likely (NPV 0.97)
STESS 2 — FAVORABLE: ~3% in-hospital mortality; survival highly likely (NPV 0.97)
STESS 3 — UNFAVORABLE: ~39% in-hospital mortality (low PPV — do not use to justify withdrawing care)
STESS 4 — UNFAVORABLE: ~39% in-hospital mortality (low PPV — do not use to justify withdrawing care)
STESS 5 — UNFAVORABLE: ~39% in-hospital mortality (low PPV — do not use to justify withdrawing care)
STESS 6 — UNFAVORABLE: ~39% in-hospital mortality (low PPV — do not use to justify withdrawing care)

model.note: STESS is validated as a two-band stratifier, not a per-point probability: 0–2 favorable (~3% in-hospital mortality, NPV 0.97) vs 3–6 unfavorable (~39%). Band mortality from Rossetti 2008 (Table 4, 154 patients). Its value is ruling OUT death (high NPV); its PPV is low (0.39), so an unfavorable score must not justify withdrawal of care.
model.viz_info: Consciousness, worst seizure type, age and seizure history each add points (waterfall). A total of 0–2 is favorable (~3% in-hospital mortality); 3–6 is unfavorable (~39%). The donut shows the band's mortality. STESS reliably identifies survivors (high NPV) but does not reliably predict death (low PPV).

provenance.source: STESS point table taken verbatim from Rossetti et al., J Neurol 2008, Table 1 (doi 10.1007/s00415-008-0989-1, PMID 18769858; PDF in 0_inbox/papers/status_epilepticus/). Band mortality (0–2 → 3%, 3–6 → 39%) and NPV 0.97 / PPV 0.39 from Table 4 of the same paper (154 patients).
provenance.notes: 4 predictors exactly per Table 1: consciousness (0/1), worst seizure type (0/1/2), age (0/2), history of previous seizures (0/1); total 0–6. STESS validates a DICHOTOMY (0–2 favorable vs 3–6 unfavorable), so per-score rows carry the band mortality (not a spurious per-point curve). Honesty caveat (high NPV, low PPV, do not use to withdraw care) surfaced in cff_info + note per the paper's Discussion.
